items.q: When does 'in' take the accusative vs dative?
items.correct: Accusative for movement INTO a place; dative for location IN a place.
items.misconception: It always takes the same case.
items.random_wrong: It depends on the speaker's mood.
items.why: Students don't realise two-way prepositions exist. Wechselpräpositionen change case by meaning: 'Ich gehe in die Stadt' (motion, accusative); 'Ich bin in der Stadt' (location, dative).

items.q: How do you say 'I watch TV every evening' in German?
items.why: Students keep separable verbs together. FERNSEHEN splits in main clauses: sehe...fern. The prefix 'fern' goes to the END of the clause. Same with anrufen (rufe...an), aufstehen (stehe...auf), einkaufen (kaufe...ein).

items.q: What case does 'helfen' (to help) require for its object?
items.why: Students assume direct objects are always accusative. Certain German verbs (helfen, danken, gratulieren, folgen, antworten) take DATIVE objects despite appearing transitive in English.
The answer examiners want is: -e. “-en” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 2006. “1990” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately what % of Germans have a Migrationshintergrund (nearest 5)?
items.why: The real figure is 25 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
